content.thinking:
The question is asking if cannibalism typically results in the death of the victim.

Cannibalism is the act of consuming the flesh of one's own species. In the case of humans eating humans, this can involve:
1. Eating someone who is already dead (necrophagy)
2. Killing someone to eat them

So cannibalism doesn't necessarily require killing - it could involve eating someone already deceased. However, in many cases throughout history, cannibalism has involved killing the victim first.

The question asks if it "typically" results in death. I think this is asking about whether the act of cannibalism itself results in death, or whether cannibalism typically involves victims who die.

If we're talking about whether cannibalism typically involves eating people who have died or been killed - yes, that's generally the case, as you typically can't consume someone's flesh extensively while they're alive without killing them.

However, technically cannibalism could occur on already-dead bodies without causing their death. But in most contexts and historical/criminal cases, cannibalism does involve or result in the death of the victim.

I think the most accurate answer here is "yes" - cannibalism typically does result in or involve the death of the victim.
